topic: Philosophy of Religion: Arguments based on Observation & Reason
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Is 'everything has a cause, so what caused God?' a fair summary of Aquinas?
items.correct: No - Aquinas argues that things in motion or contingent things need explanation, concluding in a necessary, uncaused being; the argument never claims literally everything has a cause.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument refutes itself because God would also need a cause.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas argued the universe has no cause of any kind.
items.why: The popular one-line refutation attacks a strawman; engaging the contingency/necessity distinction is where the real debate lies.

items.q: Did Kant's objection show that God does not exist?
items.correct: No - Kant argued only that the ontological argument fails as a proof; he himself affirmed God on moral grounds elsewhere in his philosophy.
items.misconception: Kant's 'existence is not a predicate' is a disproof of God's existence.
items.random_wrong: Kant claimed predicates do not exist in grammar.
items.why: Refuting an argument for a conclusion is not refuting the conclusion - a distinction central to evaluating all these arguments.

items.q: Would a successful design argument prove the Christian God?
items.correct: Not by itself - Hume noted the evidence might suggest many designers, an apprentice designer or an impersonal force; bridging from 'a designer' to the God of classical theism needs further argument.
items.misconception: If design is established, the God of Christianity is thereby established.
items.random_wrong: The design argument is about the design of church buildings.
items.why: The gap between the argument's conclusion and full theism is one of Hume's sharpest and most examinable points.

items.q: Which argument for God's existence is based on reason alone (a priori)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The ontological argument. “The teleological argument” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Aquinas's third Way argues from what?
items.correct: Contingency - contingent things require a necessary being
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Contingency - contingent things require a necessary being. “The beauty of nature” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was Gaunilo's objection to Anselm?
items.correct: The same logic could 'prove' a perfect island exists, so the argument must be flawed
items.misconception: God is too holy to be discussed
items.random_wrong: Islands are more perfect than God
items.why: It's The same logic could 'prove' a perfect island exists, so the argument must be flawed. “God is too holy to be discussed” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why did Hume think the design analogy was weak?
items.correct: The universe is unlike human artefacts, and we have no experience of universes being made
items.misconception: Watches do not really need designers
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The universe is unlike human artefacts, and we have no experience of universes being made. “Watches do not really need designers” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
